author: @NoahNobody
text: Very cool. First time I've heard of a floating bearing.

author: @1mremington
text: Nice job! The floating bearing also serves as a flywheel, of sorts.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah I suppose so - didn't really think about it but now you mention it - that makes sense to me - cheers

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: Hi Robert, I had a rather heated discussion in my lunchbreak with my boss why oldfashioned windmill's in the Netherlands always turn anti clockwise as seen from the front. I think it was just a standardisation so all would use the same parts. My Boss thought Northern hemisphere has something to do with it.  I hope it's an inspirational question. If you could make a video on the subject that would be great. Otherwise I would like your opinion on the subject ? Haven't done any research myself as I'm sure you'll have an answer. I'm not sure you had any historical windmills in the UK. Maybe.  In holland these are still maintained as a hobby for some people and even put to use for small scale shops.

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text:
Some of them are still used to pump the sea water out!!🙂
At least they Did, 
~when I was still living there,,,
I moved to the UK when I was 7yrs old,,,
 and I suppose that was quite a long time ago....😳
Namaste 🙏
Andrea and Critters. .XxX..💞
Ps, That IS a Very good Question!!
I think that it was a standardized thing, but also because Anti-clockwise has "Sinister" connections, maybe the Church had something to do with this?!😁💞

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: @AndreaDingbatt not today i think. But mills for wheat and corn are still maintained for old times sake

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think you are right mate - it's to do with the pitch of the sail - and I don't doubt the twist is standard - from parts available and historically how to make them was taught - but that's a guess on my part and I can always give it a try

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
@hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thanks for the comment mate. Even the milling stone had a carved pattern on it and will only mill one way. Standardisation was likely the reason.

author: @NeoShameMan
text: I have been wondering, how many way there is to make clear lenses from scratch without glass? Though I'm starting to think polish metal can probably be enough for solar collector and even full telescope like contraception.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I made them out of water mate

replies.author: @NeoShameMan
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I have seen the video! But you use a clear plastic holder!  I'm on an small island, if supply stop due to global black Swan event like covid, plastic will degrade quickly over the years. I tried that already.
